subject: PhD Logic
instructions: Solve 5 of the following problems; at least one from problems 1–3, at least one from problems 4–6, and at least one from problems 7–9. Problems 1–3 concern Set Theory; problems 4–6 concern Computability; problems 7–9 concern Model Theory.

problems.text: Let \(X\) be an uncountable set and let \(F:X\to X\) be a function. Show that there are distinct elements \(x,y\in X\) such that \(x\ne F(y)\) and \(y\ne F(x)\).
Let \(P\) be a forcing. Show that there is an uncountable cardinal \(\kappa\) such that \(P\Vdash \check{\kappa}\text{ is a cardinal}\).
Provide an example of a subset of a Polish space which is analytic and not Borel. Provide a proof of these two properties of your set.
Give an example of a subset of \(\mathbb{N}\) that is \(\Pi^0_1\) and not \(\Sigma^0_1\) (and prove that your set has these two properties).
Prove that there exist incomparable Turing degrees.
Prove that there exist disjoint computably enumerable sets \(A,B\subseteq\mathbb{N}\) such that there is no computable set \(C\) which contains \(A\) and is disjoint from \(B\).

Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a language comprised of a single binary relation symbol. Give an example of a countably infinite \(\mathcal{L}\)-structure \(\mathcal{M}\) such that the following hold. Make sure to prove both statements (i) and (ii).

Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be a language comprised of two unary relation symbols \(G\) and \(B\), and a binary relation symbol \(R\). Let \(\mathcal{K}\) be the class of all finite graphs with a bipartition, i.e., \(\mathcal{K}\) consists of all finite \(\mathcal{L}\)-structures \(\mathcal{M}=(M,G^{\mathcal{M}},B^{\mathcal{M}},R^{\mathcal{M}})\) such that the following hold. Prove or disprove: \(\mathcal{K}\) is a Fraïssé class.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: \(R^{\mathcal{M}}\) is a symmetric and irreflexive relation on \(M\),

subparts.label: (iii)
subparts.text: if \((x,y)\in R^{\mathcal{M}}\), then exactly one of \(x,y\) belongs to \(G^{\mathcal{M}}\) and the other belongs to \(B^{\mathcal{M}}\).

problems.text: Let \(\mathcal{L}=\{R\}\) be the language of graphs (i.e., \(R\) is a binary relation symbol). Let \(\mathcal{K}_0\) be the class of all connected graphs, and let \(\mathcal{K}_1\) be the class of all disconnected graphs. Prove or disprove each of the following:
